source.title: TwinLeaf - Embedding fanout + hosting evaluation (discovery)
next_steps: Aisha to share pilot pricing sheet and dedicated capacity options
Marcus to propose throughput test plan and example payloads
TwinLeaf to provide sample dataset (10k docs) and SLAs for P0 endpoints
Schedule follow-up technical deep dive (SE-led) week of 2029-03-11
action_items: Aisha: send pricing + pilot contract draft by 2029-03-07
Marcus: bench test plan + sample curl commands by 2029-03-08
Ravi: share sample indexing data and target QPS by 2029-03-06
Elena: confirm VPC peering and KMS provider by 2029-03-10
source.transcript:
Meeting header
Date: 2029-03-05
Time: 16:00 UTC
Duration: ~48 minutes
Attendees: Aisha (Redwood AE), Marcus (Redwood SE), Ravi (TwinLeaf Head of Search), Elena (Platform), Jonas (CTO), Sara (SRE), Mike (Data Scientist)

Auto-summary (auto-generated, may be rough):
- TwinLeaf is evaluating whether to use Redwood Hosted for initial rollout or go Dedicated/Private for low-latency reranking.
- Main concerns: embed dimensions and storage cost, fanout to vector DB, tail latency at 99.9, integration with existing FAISS/Chroma workflows, VPC/KMS/compliance.
- Agreed next step: pilot dataset and bench plan; Redwood to share pricing and test scaffolding.

Topics: embeddings dimensionality, hybrid search + rerank, hosting options (hosted vs dedicated vs private), vector DB fallback/fanout, throughput and tail latency, security (VPC, KMS, SOC2).

Transcript body:
[00:00] Aisha: hey everyone thanks for carving time today um quick intros on our side im aisha i run enterprise for redwood and with me is marcus lin, our solutions engineer.
[00:10] Marcus: hey all, hi hi.
[00:12] Ravi: hi Aisha, Marcus, ravi here. thanks.
[00:15] Jonas: jonas, excited to dig in.
[00:18] Elena: elena, platform.
[00:20] Sara: sara, s r e.
[00:21] Mike: mike, data science.
[00:24] Aisha: awesome quick agenda - we want to understand your current retrieval stack, your latency and qps targets, how you do rerank today, and then walk through where redwood fits (hosted, dedicated, private). then next steps. sound good?
[00:35] Ravi: yeah that's perfect. to start high level we run hybrid search today - dense vectors via FAISS in our infra plus a term-based index. we do a lightweight lexical filter then vector fanout to get top 200 then rerank with a 1.3B model for contextual scoring. goal is sub-200ms p95 on the rerank path, p99 ideally under 350ms.
[00:56] Marcus: ok that's helpful. when you say fanout to top 200 is that synchronous? and where do embeddings live, in-chunked s3 or hot cache?
[01:05] Ravi: synchronous fanout. embeddings in S3 but cached in a hot redis for the last week; we also pre-shard some heavy tables into faiss nodes.
[01:12] Marcus: got it. so two orthogonal concerns we see: 1) where to compute and serve embeddings and rerank — hosted (Redwood API) vs dedicated pools or private (VPC/on-prem) — and 2) where vector search lives (we integrate with external vector DBs or use our fallback).
[01:27] Mike: quick question - what's the overhead if we keep embeddings at 1,536 dims vs compress to 768? cost delta and, uh, quality tradeoffs?
[01:35] Marcus: good question. broadly 1,536 will give you higher recall for some semantic tasks at the cost of ~2x storage and slightly more compute per query on nearest-neighbor. we also recommend evaluating compact embeddings or PQ (product quantization) in the vector DB — redwood's runtime supports serving either full or quantized payloads and will do KV cache for repeated prefixes to cut token cost. apologies i said KV cache — k v — speaks weird on transcript.
[02:00] Elena: on hosting, our security team wants VPC peering and customer-managed KMS at minimum. SOC2 is table stakes. if we go hosted we need to know the isolation and egress patterns. can you run through hosted vs dedicated vs private?
[02:14] Aisha: sure. hosted is fastest to get started — you call redwood's API, we manage models and autoscaling. Dedicated is reserved GPU pools with capacity isolation and better SLOs; you get per-customer pools and can restrict models and traffic routes. Private gives you a customer-managed control plane — VPC peering, on-prem option, and support for your KMS/HSM. we can provide SOC2 artifacts and audit logs for all tiers; private gives the most control for egress and encryption.
[02:45] Sara: and latency-wise? we care about tail. our current p99 hits 420ms occasionally during peaks.
[02:52] Marcus: with Dedicated we often hit p95 sub-120ms for small rerank models when batching is tuned; p99 depends on your topology — network hop and vector DB fanout are usually the largest contributors. if you colocate rerank with the vector shards (same vpc/dedicated nodes) you cut that network tail.
[03:10] Ravi: right that's our question — do we route fanout from our faiss to redwood for rerank, or push data into redwood's vector subsystem? we have compliance limits around PII leaving our VPC.
[03:22] Marcus: two patterns we've seen: A) keep vector DB in your environment, call it to return ids, then call redwood for rerank only (embedding + model call), or B) push embeddings into a managed vector DB (we integrate with pinecone/chroma/weaviate) and let redwood orchestrate retrieval + rerank. pattern A minimizes data transfer but adds network hops; pattern B reduces latency but requires you to accept storing vectors in the managed DB (or use private deployment).
[03:45] Mike: also curious about fallback strategy on failures — if a vector DB times out do you have partial rerank or degrade gracefully?
[03:51] Marcus: yes we support fallback topologies — e.g., try primary vector DB, fallback to smaller in-memory index or prefix-cache within the runtime, then optionally fall back to lexical-only results. these are configurable routing policies. another trick is locally mirroring topk candidates to a small faiss in your app for emergency failover.
[04:10] Jonas: what about throughput — we are targeting sustained 600 QPS on retrieval+rerank during business hours with spikes to 2k. how would dedicated scale?
[04:20] Marcus: for that scale i'd recommend Dedicated with autoscaling pools sized to sustain baseline; we can size GPUs/replicas based on your model (1.3B reranker vs 7B) and sequence length. important knob: batching windows — larger batch improves throughput but can increase latency. we model cost/perf and can share sizing guidance.
[04:40] Ravi: can you quantify cost for the two options? even ballpark per 1M queries?
[04:44] Aisha: i'll follow up with a pricing matrix — hosted per-token/embedding; dedicated is a commit + unit rate; private is enterprise license. we'll include example math for 1.3B rerank at your QPS.
[04:56] Elena: we also use a home-grown annotation set for evals. how do we prevent regressions when you update model versions? do you have eval hooks?
[05:04] Marcus: yes Redwood Optimize has eval and rollout integration — you can register prompt/eval sets and get regression alerts. for pilots we suggest pinning the model and doing canary rollouts while watching quality metrics.
[05:15] Mike: re embeddings — if we reduce dims to 768 and use HNSW in Faiss, what does bank of weights look like for rerank? sorry phrase — what's the impact on downstream reranker?
[05:24] Marcus: shorter vectors can slightly reduce recall; reranker often compensates if candidate set still has positives. solid strategy: small-time A/B: compare 768 vs 1536 on your dev queries and sample user traffic. for production you might do 1536 for long-tail doc types and 768 for conversation-level caching.
[05:39] Aisha: quick check — do you have any hard compliance timelines or procurement windows?
[05:43] Jonas: ideally pilot in next 3 weeks and production Q3. procurement wants an NDA + standard security pack.
[05:49] Aisha: we can share SOC2, ISO artifacts and an initial MSA.
[05:53] Sara: on VPC peering — we use AWS, prefer privatelink where possible. does Redwood support privatelink or direct connect?
[05:59] Marcus: we support VPC peering and privatelink for Private and some Dedicated setups; direct connect can be arranged for Private/on-prem. we'll capture your network team requirements in the follow-up.
[06:10] Ravi: can we talk about durability and vector sync? if we push to your managed DB and you have replication, how do we ensure parity with our primary store?
[06:20] Marcus: common approach is to have incremental syncs with event logs; for strict parity customers run a short-lived verification job. if precise one-to-one is required, Private deployment or Dedicated with VPC reduces the operational surface.
[06:34] Mike: any gotchas with tokenization differences or special characters in our legal docs that affect embeddings?
[06:38] Marcus: tokenizer differences only matter for generation models; embeddings are mostly model-dependent. we do recommend normalizing some fields and removing boilerplate. also watch out for duplicate doc passages which can skew nearest neighbor.
[06:52] Elena: what about monitoring? we need traces from request down to vector DB calls.
[06:57] Marcus: Redwood Console exposes per-route latency, per-token cost, and tracing hooks. you can push traces to your observability backend or integrate with our trace IDs. error budgets and SLO dashboards are available on Dedicated and Private tiers.
[07:12] Ravi: is there an easy way to evaluate tail latency before committing? a smoke test?
[07:17] Marcus: yes we do a bench test — you provide sample payloads and we run end-to-end scenarios under load (fanout size, vector DB latency injection, rerank model size). we'll give you p50/p95/p99 numbers and a breakdown.
[07:29] Jonas: are there reference customers running a similar hybrid fanout pattern?
[07:33] Aisha: yes we've got several retail and search customers doing async fanout + rerank with dedicated pools; we can share anonymized results and a short case study.
[07:40] Sara: latency to europe/asia — we have global footprint; do you have region support?
[07:45] Marcus: yes hosted has multi-region endpoints; Dedicated can be provisioned in specific regions; Private obviously depends on your infra.
[07:52] Mika (noise, mislabel): [crosstalk] 
[07:55] Mike: one other concern — model quantization for rerank, do you support int8 / q4 variants?
[07:59] Marcus: we support quantized variants for many architectures and can recommend quant profiles based on quality tests. quantization can reduce memory and increase throughput but you should evaluate on your eval set.
[08:12] Ravi: ok this is helpful. so recap from our side: we want a bench test with our 10k doc sample, keep faiss in our VPC for pilot (pattern A) but test an alternate where we push vectors into managed DB to compare latency; we'll also test dims 768 vs 1536, and run p95/p99 targets under 600 sustained qps and spikes.
[08:30] Aisha: perfect. recap from redwood side: we'll send pilot pricing, bench plan, and security pack. we'll schedule se-deep dive next week to align on network and sample exchange.
[08:40] Jonas: thanks everyone.
[08:42] Marcus: thanks, i'll follow up with curl samples and a draft bench script.
[08:46] Aisha: thanks all. take care.

End of transcript

References and links noted in call:
- Pilot pricing spreadsheet (link shared after call)
- Redwood security pack (SOC2/ISO + network checklist)
- Bench test scaffold (GitHub gist link to sample load script)
- Example case study (retail fanout + dedicated)

Note: transcript contains some transcription errors, occasional speaker mislabels, and filler words.

Time: 16:00 UTC

[06:38] Marcus: tokenizer differences only matter for generation models; embeddings are mostly model-dependent. we do recommend normalizing some fields and removing boilerplate. also watch out for duplicate doc passages which can skew nearest neighbor.
[06:52] Elena: what about monitoring? we need traces from request down to vector DB calls.
[06:57] Marcus: Redwood Console exposes per-route latency, per-token cost, and tracing hooks. you can push traces to your observability backend or integrate with our trace IDs. error budgets and SLO dashboards are available on Dedicated and Private tiers.
[07:12] Ravi: is there an easy way to evaluate tail latency before committing? a smoke test?
[07:17] Marcus: yes we do a bench test — you provide sample payloads and we run end-to-end scenarios under load (fanout size, vector DB latency injection, rerank model size). we'll give you p50/p95/p99 numbers and a breakdown.
[07:29] Jonas: are there reference customers running a similar hybrid fanout pattern?
[07:33] Aisha: yes we've got several retail and search customers doing async fanout + rerank with dedicated pools; we can share anonymized results and a short case study.
[07:40] Sara: latency to europe/asia — we have global footprint; do you have region support?
[07:45] Marcus: yes hosted has multi-region endpoints; Dedicated can be provisioned in specific regions; Private obviously depends on your infra.
[07:52] Mika (noise, mislabel): [crosstalk] 
[07:55] Mike: one other concern — model quantization for rerank, do you support int8 / q4 variants?
[07:59] Marcus: we support quantized variants for many architectures and can recommend quant profiles based on quality tests. quantization can reduce memory and increase throughput but you should evaluate on your eval set.
[08:12] Ravi: ok this is helpful. so recap from our side: we want a bench test with our 10k doc sample, keep faiss in our VPC for pilot (pattern A) but test an alternate where we push vectors into managed DB to compare latency; we'll also test dims 768 vs 1536, and run p95/p99 targets under 600 sustained qps and spikes.
[08:30] Aisha: perfect. recap from redwood side: we'll send pilot pricing, bench plan, and security pack. we'll schedule se-deep dive next week to align on network and sample exchange.
[08:40] Jonas: thanks everyone.
[08:42] Marcus: thanks, i'll follow up with curl samples and a draft bench script.
[08:46] Aisha: thanks all. take care.

End of transcript

References and links noted in call:
- Pilot pricing spreadsheet (link shared after call)
- Redwood security pack (SOC2/ISO + network checklist)
- Bench test scaffold (GitHub gist link to sample load script)
- Example case study (retail fanout + dedicated)

Note: transcript contains some transcription errors, occasional speaker mislabels, and filler words.

Priya to share a short benchmark run (small sample) on hosted vs dedicated latency
Customer to confirm expected QPS and data retention policy by 2025-04-22
Schedule follow-up deep-dive on VPC/private deployment with TwinLeaf infra team

source.transcript:
Meeting Header
Date: 2025-04-18 16:00 PDT
Duration: ~52 minutes
Attendees: Jordan Blake (Redwood AE), Priya Nair (Redwood SE), Marcus Chen (TwinLeaf), Aisha Rodriguez (TwinLeaf), Tomás Alvarez (TwinLeaf)
Recording: auto-generated
Shared artifacts: Dual-lang quickstart repo (short link provided in chat), Redwood console demo deck (link), bench spreadsheet referenced: /drive/TwinLeaf-redwood-bench.xlsx

Summary:
Quick 1:1 handoff focused on SDK integration in two languages (Python + TypeScript), CI secrets best practices, environment bootstrapping, and a small perf sanity check between hosted and dedicated. Customer has PoC target in 6 weeks, wants CI examples and notes on private deployment constraints.

Topics:
- Quickstart walkthrough (Python + TS)
- Auth patterns and CI secret injection
- Env variables, local dev vs CI, and docker examples
- Performance expectations and batching knobs in Redwood Optimize
- Next steps: repo, small benchmark, private deployment follow-up

Transcript Body:
[00:00] Jordan: Hey everyone, thanks for joining, I think we have about 50 minutes. Uh, quick intros? I'll kick off — Jordan Blake, AE at Redwood.
[00:10] Marcus: Hi, Marcus Chen, platform eng at TwinLeaf. Trying to get a clean CI + SDK path for our feature teams.
[00:16] Aisha: Aisha, ML eng, mostly model infra and embeddings pipelines.
[00:22] Tomás: Tomás here, SRE, will be the one looking at network & secrets.
[00:28] Priya: Priya Nair, solutions engineer on the infra team, I'll do the deep-dive on SDK and config.
[00:34] Jordan: Cool. High level agenda: run through the quickstart in both languages, show the auth patterns we recommend for CI, talk through environment setup and a brief mention of Redwood Optimize tuning for batching. Any additions?
[00:46] Marcus: Sounds good. Important for us: we want to avoid hard-coding keys, and we use GitHub Actions and HashiCorp Vault primarily. Also need to know when we should move from hosted to dedicated capacity.
[00:58] Priya: Got it — we'll show a CI secret snippet and also the small fallback pattern for when dedicated not available.
[01:05] Jordan: Quick housekeeping, shared repo link in chat: tinyurl.com/rlwd-dual-quickstart (note: not the final org repo).
[01:14] Priya: Alright, starting with Python. We'll open the sample app — it's intentionally minimal. Requirements: python 3.10+, pip install redwood-client (example name), set REDWOOD_API_KEY as env. Code snippet: import redwood; client = redwood.Client(api_key=os.getenv('REDWOOD_API_KEY'))... then generate.
[01:35] Aisha: Quick Q: do we have an official python package name? I saw redwood-sdk in docs.
[01:40] Jordan: There's a naming mismatch sometimes in notes — current pip is redwood-inference-client in org packages, but the quickstart uses a stable alias for clarity. We'll include exact pip name in the README.
[01:52] Priya: For TypeScript, similar pattern: npm i @redwood/inference, use process.env.REDWOOD_API_KEY, and we demo both fetch-based and the official Node SDK that supports streaming.
[02:05] Marcus: When you say streaming, do you mean SSE or web socket?
[02:09] Priya: SSE/HTTP chunked responses for the hosted API; Node SDK wraps the stream into an async iterator. We'll show the small code snippet.
[02:20] Jordan: Let's jump to CI secrets and avoid the common pitfalls. Tomás, you said you use Vault?
[02:26] Tomás: Yeah Vault for production secrets, but GH Actions for lower envs. We want a pattern that plays nice with both.
[02:33] Priya: Recommended: don't check in keys, use ephemeral secrets if possible. For GH Actions we show an example with secrets.REDWOOD_API_KEY and a small wrapper step to inject into the job env. For Vault we show a short script to pull and export REDWOOD_API_KEY for the runner.
[02:50] Jordan: Example GH Actions snippet in the repo:
- name: Set REDWOOD key
  env:
    REDWOOD_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.REDWOOD_API_KEY }}
  run: echo 'REDWOOD_API_KEY set'

[03:05] Aisha: For local dev, should engineers create scoped keys per developer or use a shared dev key?
[03:12] Priya: We usually recommend scoped dev keys per engineer when possible; for orgs that want lower friction, a shared dev key with IP-based limits can work. Also, set low quotas on dev keys to limit abuse.
[03:26] Marcus: Ok, that's helpful. What about rate limits and batching? Our prod QPS target is about 60 requests/sec but most are short prompts with embeddings and small chat.
[03:36] Priya: That maps well — Redwood Optimize has batching suggestions: you can set preferred max_batch_tokens and preferred latency targets. For 60 QPS of short prompts, continuous batching with small max latency (20-50ms) is the usual starting point. We'll also include a recommended initial config in the repo.
[03:54] Tomás: Is there an easy way to toggle between hosted and dedicated endpoints? If we reserve Dedicated later, do we need to change code?
[04:01] Jordan: No code change usually — endpoint abstraction in SDK supports routing by policy. You can set a config variable or environment flag and the SDK will use a different base_url. We show that in the sample with REDWOOD_ENV=hosted|dedicated.
[04:14] Marcus: Nice. Anyone else using SSO/key rotation? We will need to rotate keys programmatically.
[04:19] Priya: Yes, we recommend short-lived tokens for high-security envs and integrate with your CI to fetch tokens. We'll link the token exchange doc in the repo.
[04:28] Jordan: Quick demo: I'll share my screen and walk the Python quickstart run locally so you can see logs and the env pattern.
[04:36] [screen share noise]
[04:42] Priya: You might see a slight transcription glitch but colors are fine — code run: python main.py -> calls the API and prints output. Note: logs redact the API key by default in SDK.
[04:58] Aisha: We noticed earlier that Redwood Optimize produces config recs — is that automatic or do we have to run something?
[05:05] Priya: Optimize can run in two modes: automatic suggestion based on observed workload (if you opt in), or manual evaluation where you upload a small trace and we return recommendations. For PoC, manual is faster — send over 1000 requests sample and we return batching + quantization suggestions.
[05:23] Marcus: Great. We have some custom LLMs too — can you host our model in Dedicated or must it be in Private?
[05:30] Jordan: We support serving customer-owned models in Redwood Dedicated and in Redwood Private deployments. For on-prem or air-gapped, Private is the route. Dedicated can host private models if they are uploaded to a secure tenant, but if you have strict data residency or no egress, Private is better. We'll need to discuss network topology.
[05:46] Tomás: On that note, what kind of KMS integrations do you support? We use AWS KMS and HashiCorp HSM in some regions.
[05:55] Priya: AWS KMS, Google KMS, and customer-managed HSMs via our Private offering — we'll attach the security docs, but in short, keys can be customer-managed for Private and Dedicated with appropriate RBAC.
[06:10] Jordan: Returning to quickstart, I want to highlight CI snippet for tests: run a smoke test using a low-cost model and mock tokens or a test key to avoid billing surprises. Example in repo includes a 'smoke' workflow.
[06:26] Aisha: Do you have a test stub or a local emulator?
[06:30] Priya: We have a local offline mode that returns canned responses for dev tests; it's not a full emulator but useful for CI unit tests. Note: it's not suitable for perf benchmarks.
[06:40] Marcus: We'll use that for unit tests but we'll need the real hosted endpoint for our integration tests.
[06:44] Jordan: Let's run the Node quickstart briefly. The Node sample shows streaming and structured output patterns (JSON schema enforcement).
[06:51] [background typing]
[07:04] Priya: Node snippet: const client = new Redwood({ apiKey: process.env.REDWOOD_API_KEY }); await client.chat.stream(...). The stream yields tokens and you can pipe directly to the browser or server-sent events.
[07:18] Marcus: Any gotchas around Node version or ESM vs CJS?
[07:22] Priya: We support both, but streaming requires Node 18+ for stable fetch/stream behavior. We call that out in the README.
[07:33] Aisha: Could you talk about embeddings flow? We plan to store vectors in our vector DB and want guidance on normalization and tokenization.
[07:40] Priya: For embeddings, we suggest batching vector calls, set a max_batch_size and lower latency threshold. Normalize text by lowercasing and stripping certain punctuation unless you need exact case. We'll include a suggested preproc snippet in the repo.
[07:56] Jordan: Ok, quick pivot to perf — Priya, can you show the small benchmark config you mentioned?
[08:02] Priya: Sure, it's a script in tools/bench that calls the same prompt payload 100 times with concurrency 8, captures p50/p95, and posts results as CSV. For the PoC, we'll run this on hosted and on a small dedicated node to compare. We'll send the results.
[08:20] Tomás: How long does it take to spin up a dedicated pool for a small PoC?
[08:25] Jordan: Depends on capacity, but typically 24-72 hours once the contract is in place. For immediate testing we do hosted with reserved burst credits or short-term dedicated trial if capacity allows.
[08:38] Marcus: Could you outline the cost delta? Rough per-token, per-hour guidance?
[08:44] Jordan: We'll send a tailored cost estimate in the follow-up. Quick rule: dedicated amortizes GPU cost for sustained high QPS; hosted is usually cheaper short-term for variable workloads. Priya will attach a simple spreadsheet with assumptions.
[08:59] [minor crosstalk]
[09:05] Aisha: Security question — audit logs for API calls and data residency controls?
[09:10] Priya: Audit logs available in Console, retains events per your plan. For data residency, Private supports on-prem and region pinning. We'll include SOC2 & ISO attachments.
[09:23] Marcus: Perfect. We need the security pack for our compliance team.
[09:27] Jordan: We'll push the security docs to the shared link after the call.
[09:33] [silence then typing]
[09:48] Priya: Recap of the technical bits we covered: Python + TS quickstart, CI secret patterns (GH Actions + Vault), dev vs CI keys, local offline mode for unit tests, small perf bench script, notes on hosted vs dedicated and private.
[09:59] Marcus: That's comprehensive. For next steps, we'll want the quickstart repo, the GH Actions snippet, and a small benchmark run from you.
[10:07] Jordan: Yep, action items recorded. Any remaining questions?
[10:12] Tomás: One last one: any recommended Redis or KV cache patterns for prompting large context windows?
[10:18] Priya: For KV cache we recommend prefix caching and TTLs keyed by conversation session. Avoid caching model outputs unless deterministic. We'll include a short pattern doc.
[10:28] Aisha: Also, if we choose quantized models later, is there any SDK change?
[10:32] Priya: No SDK change, just model selection and possibly different latency profiles; Optimize will suggest quantization when it's safe for your evals.
[10:40] Marcus: Great. Thanks everyone.
[10:44] Jordan: Thanks TwinLeaf. We'll send the repo, CI snippets, and run the 100-request bench and follow up. Meeting over.

End of transcript.

Notes on transcript quality: Auto-transcription had occasional name/case issues (Tomás transcribed as Tomas sometimes), and some product names abbreviated. There are a couple of places where audio overlapped and words were dropped.

[02:33] Priya: Recommended: don't check in keys, use ephemeral secrets if possible. For GH Actions we show an example with secrets.REDWOOD_API_KEY and a small wrapper step to inject into the job env. For Vault we show a short script to pull and export REDWOOD_API_KEY for the runner.
[02:50] Jordan: Example GH Actions snippet in the repo:
- name: Set REDWOOD key
  env:
    REDWOOD_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.REDWOOD_API_KEY }}
  run: echo 'REDWOOD_API_KEY set'

[03:05] Aisha: For local dev, should engineers create scoped keys per developer or use a shared dev key?
[03:12] Priya: We usually recommend scoped dev keys per engineer when possible; for orgs that want lower friction, a shared dev key with IP-based limits can work. Also, set low quotas on dev keys to limit abuse.
[03:26] Marcus: Ok, that's helpful. What about rate limits and batching? Our prod QPS target is about 60 requests/sec but most are short prompts with embeddings and small chat.
[03:36] Priya: That maps well — Redwood Optimize has batching suggestions: you can set preferred max_batch_tokens and preferred latency targets. For 60 QPS of short prompts, continuous batching with small max latency (20-50ms) is the usual starting point. We'll also include a recommended initial config in the repo.
[03:54] Tomás: Is there an easy way to toggle between hosted and dedicated endpoints? If we reserve Dedicated later, do we need to change code?
[04:01] Jordan: No code change usually — endpoint abstraction in SDK supports routing by policy. You can set a config variable or environment flag and the SDK will use a different base_url. We show that in the sample with REDWOOD_ENV=hosted|dedicated.
[04:14] Marcus: Nice. Anyone else using SSO/key rotation? We will need to rotate keys programmatically.
[04:19] Priya: Yes, we recommend short-lived tokens for high-security envs and integrate with your CI to fetch tokens. We'll link the token exchange doc in the repo.
[04:28] Jordan: Quick demo: I'll share my screen and walk the Python quickstart run locally so you can see logs and the env pattern.
[04:36] [screen share noise]
[04:42] Priya: You might see a slight transcription glitch but colors are fine — code run: python main.py -> calls the API and prints output. Note: logs redact the API key by default in SDK.
[04:58] Aisha: We noticed earlier that Redwood Optimize produces config recs — is that automatic or do we have to run something?
[05:05] Priya: Optimize can run in two modes: automatic suggestion based on observed workload (if you opt in), or manual evaluation where you upload a small trace and we return recommendations. For PoC, manual is faster — send over 1000 requests sample and we return batching + quantization suggestions.
[05:23] Marcus: Great. We have some custom LLMs too — can you host our model in Dedicated or must it be in Private?
[05:30] Jordan: We support serving customer-owned models in Redwood Dedicated and in Redwood Private deployments. For on-prem or air-gapped, Private is the route. Dedicated can host private models if they are uploaded to a secure tenant, but if you have strict data residency or no egress, Private is better. We'll need to discuss network topology.
[05:46] Tomás: On that note, what kind of KMS integrations do you support? We use AWS KMS and HashiCorp HSM in some regions.
[05:55] Priya: AWS KMS, Google KMS, and customer-managed HSMs via our Private offering — we'll attach the security docs, but in short, keys can be customer-managed for Private and Dedicated with appropriate RBAC.
[06:10] Jordan: Returning to quickstart, I want to highlight CI snippet for tests: run a smoke test using a low-cost model and mock tokens or a test key to avoid billing surprises. Example in repo includes a 'smoke' workflow.
[06:26] Aisha: Do you have a test stub or a local emulator?
[06:30] Priya: We have a local offline mode that returns canned responses for dev tests; it's not a full emulator but useful for CI unit tests. Note: it's not suitable for perf benchmarks.

[07:04] Priya: Node snippet: const client = new Redwood({ apiKey: process.env.REDWOOD_API_KEY }); await client.chat.stream(...). The stream yields tokens and you can pipe directly to the browser or server-sent events.
[07:18] Marcus: Any gotchas around Node version or ESM vs CJS?
[07:22] Priya: We support both, but streaming requires Node 18+ for stable fetch/stream behavior. We call that out in the README.
[07:33] Aisha: Could you talk about embeddings flow? We plan to store vectors in our vector DB and want guidance on normalization and tokenization.
[07:40] Priya: For embeddings, we suggest batching vector calls, set a max_batch_size and lower latency threshold. Normalize text by lowercasing and stripping certain punctuation unless you need exact case. We'll include a suggested preproc snippet in the repo.

[08:02] Priya: Sure, it's a script in tools/bench that calls the same prompt payload 100 times with concurrency 8, captures p50/p95, and posts results as CSV. For the PoC, we'll run this on hosted and on a small dedicated node to compare. We'll send the results.
[08:20] Tomás: How long does it take to spin up a dedicated pool for a small PoC?
[08:25] Jordan: Depends on capacity, but typically 24-72 hours once the contract is in place. For immediate testing we do hosted with reserved burst credits or short-term dedicated trial if capacity allows.
[08:38] Marcus: Could you outline the cost delta? Rough per-token, per-hour guidance?
[08:44] Jordan: We'll send a tailored cost estimate in the follow-up. Quick rule: dedicated amortizes GPU cost for sustained high QPS; hosted is usually cheaper short-term for variable workloads. Priya will attach a simple spreadsheet with assumptions.
